userStories.title: Create a test file
userStories.description: Create a simple test file to verify the agent works
acceptanceCriteria: Create test.txt with hello content
Typecheck passes

userStories.title: Log iteration progress to progress.txt during the agent loop
userStories.description: The main loop in src/core/iterator.ts never writes to progress.txt during iterations. The file is created by the Archiver with a header but remains empty throughout the run. After each iteration, the loop should append a timestamped entry to progress.txt with the iteration number, which story was targeted, and the current completion status. This makes progress.txt the human-readable run log it was designed to be, and fixes the misleading message at line 108 that says 'Check progress.txt for details' when the file has no details.
acceptanceCriteria: After each iteration in the main loop, append a timestamped entry to progress.txt with: iteration number, targeted story id/title, and completion status
When all stories complete, append a final summary entry to progress.txt
When max iterations are reached without completion, append a warning entry to progress.txt
The Archiver's initProgressFile creates the file with a header that entries can be appended to
Build passes with npm run build
Verify manually by running with --dry-run that progress.txt gets populated
userStories.notes: progress.txt is currently a dead file — created but never written to during the loop. This was found by inspecting src/core/iterator.ts lines 61-108.

userStories.title: Add --dry-run flag to test loop iteration without spawning tools
userStories.description: Add a --dry-run flag to the CLI that simulates the loop by logging what would happen at each iteration (which story would be picked, which tool would run) without actually spawning the AI tool. This allows verifying that the loop correctly picks the highest-priority incomplete story, tracks progress across iterations, and detects completion when all stories have passes:true.
acceptanceCriteria: Add --dry-run flag to the CLI in src/index.ts via commander
In dry-run mode, log which story would be picked, which tool would run, and the iteration number
Simulate completing one story per iteration (set passes:true on the next incomplete story, then reload)
Detect and log completion when all stories have passes:true
Do NOT spawn any external process in dry-run mode
Build passes with npm run build
userStories.notes: This story validates the loop mechanism. If dry-run completes successfully, the loop orchestration is working correctly.

userStories.title: Add --init flag to bootstrap agent-cli files in a target project directory
userStories.description: Add a --init flag that copies the scaffold files (agent-cli.md, progress.txt header) to a target directory specified by --directory. If prd.json already exists in the target directory, skip the copy and warn the user — they may have existing work. If it doesn't exist, copy a template prd.json with placeholder values. This lets users run `agent-cli --init --directory /path/to/project` to set up a new project quickly without manually creating the files.
acceptanceCriteria: Add --init flag to the CLI in src/index.ts via commander
When --init is used, copy agent-cli.md from the agent-cli package to the target directory
Create progress.txt with the standard header in the target directory
If prd.json already exists in the target directory, skip it and log a warning — do not overwrite
If prd.json does not exist, copy a template prd.json with placeholder project name, branchName, description, and one example userStory
Exit after init completes — do not start the agent loop
Build passes with npm run build
userStories.notes: This is a setup convenience command. The template prd.json should have clear placeholder values the user can edit.

userStories.title: Show targeted story in iteration header and remove all 'Ralph' branding
userStories.description: Two changes: (1) Before each live iteration, display the next incomplete story (id, title, priority) in the iteration header so the user knows what the agent is about to work on. (2) Remove all references to 'Ralph' across the codebase — replace with 'Agent CLI' or equivalent. Found occurrences: src/core/archiver.ts line 133 ('Ralph Progress Log'), src/utils/logger.ts line 100 (iteration header), src/core/init.ts line 51 ('Ralph Progress Log'), package.json description, and progress.txt.
acceptanceCriteria: Iteration header shows the targeted story: id, title, and priority before each live iteration starts
In dry-run mode the targeted story is also shown
Replace 'Ralph Progress Log' with 'Agent CLI Progress Log' in archiver.ts and init.ts
Replace 'Ralph Iteration' with 'Agent CLI Iteration' in logger.ts
Update package.json description to remove 'Ralph'
Build passes with npm run build
userStories.notes: Files with 'Ralph': src/core/archiver.ts:133, src/utils/logger.ts:100, src/core/init.ts:51, package.json:4

userStories.title: Show file changes with colors after each iteration
userStories.description: After each live iteration completes, detect which files changed (added, modified, removed) by comparing git status before and after the tool runs. Display the changes with chalk colors: green for added/modified, red for removed. This gives the user a clear summary of what the AI tool did in each iteration.
acceptanceCriteria: Capture git status before each live iteration starts
After the iteration, run git status again and diff the two snapshots to detect added, modified, and removed files
Display added files with green + prefix: + path/to/file.ts (added)
Display modified files with green + prefix: + path/to/file.ts (modified)
Display removed files with red - prefix: - path/to/file.ts (removed)
Show the file change summary between the iteration progress line and the next iteration header
In dry-run mode, skip file change detection (no actual changes happen)
Build passes with npm run build
userStories.notes: Use git status --porcelain to detect changes. Chalk is already a dependency.

userStories.title: Show changed files report after story completion in CLI and progress.txt
userStories.description: When a story completes (passes:true), generate a report of all files that were changed during the iterations that worked on that story. Show the report in the CLI output immediately after the completion message, and also append it to progress.txt. This gives the user a clear summary of what was done to complete each story, both on-screen and in the persistent log.
acceptanceCriteria: When a story is detected as completed (passes changed to true), collect all files changed since the story started being worked on
Display the file changes report in the CLI output right after the story completion message, using chalk colors (green for added/modified, red for removed)
Append the same file changes report to progress.txt as a section under the story completion entry
The report in progress.txt should include: story id, story title, list of changed files with their status (added/modified/removed)
In dry-run mode, skip the report (no actual changes happen)
Build passes with npm run build
userStories.notes: This builds on US-006 which tracks per-iteration changes. This story tracks cumulative changes per story across potentially multiple iterations.

userStories.title: Add 'status' command to show completed and pending stories
userStories.description: Add a `status` subcommand to the agent-cli that reads prd.json and displays a summary of how many stories are completed and how many are pending. This gives users a quick way to check progress without running the agent loop.
acceptanceCriteria: Add a `status` subcommand to the CLI in src/index.ts via commander
The command accepts --directory <path> to specify where prd.json lives (default: cwd)
Load prd.json and display: total stories, completed count, pending count
List pending stories with their id, title, and priority
If all stories are complete, show a success message
If prd.json doesn't exist, show an error
Build passes with npm run build
userStories.notes: PRDManager already has getStatus() method that returns all needed data.

userStories.title: Add JSON schema for prd.json validation
userStories.description: Create a JSON schema (prd.schema.json) that validates the prd.json format. The schema should enforce all required fields: project (string), branchName (string), description (string), and userStories (array of objects with id, title, description, acceptanceCriteria, priority, passes, notes). Update PRDManager.load() to validate prd.json against this schema on load, providing clear error messages when validation fails. IMPORTANT: maintain backward compatibility — existing prd.json files must continue to work without changes. Fields like project and branchName are typically set once and do not change during the agent loop, so the schema should not enforce mutability constraints on them.
acceptanceCriteria: Create prd.schema.json with the full schema for prd.json
Integrate schema validation into PRDManager.load() — reject invalid prd.json with a clear error message
Existing prd.json files must pass validation without any changes (backward compatible)
Schema validates required fields: project, branchName, description, userStories[]
Each userStory validates: id, title, description, acceptanceCriteria[], priority (number), passes (boolean), notes (string)
Build passes with npm run build
userStories.notes: Implemented with ajv for JSON schema validation. Schema validates all required fields with clear error messages. Backward compatible — existing prd.json passes validation.

userStories.title: Detect stale branch and clean up prd.json and progress.txt
userStories.description: When the agent loop starts, check if the branchName in prd.json still exists (git branch --list). If the branch no longer exists (was merged and deleted, or removed), the loop should: (1) clear branchName from prd.json (set to empty string or remove it), (2) log in progress.txt which branch was being worked on and that it no longer exists, (3) archive the run under that branch name, (4) stop the loop since there is no valid branch to work on. Additionally, progress.txt should always include the branchName in its header and in each iteration entry so it's clear which branch was active during execution.
acceptanceCriteria: On loop start, check if prd.json branchName exists via git branch --list
If branch no longer exists: clear branchName in prd.json, log to progress.txt which branch was stale, and stop the loop
progress.txt header includes the active branchName
Each iteration entry in progress.txt includes the branchName that was active
When a stale branch is detected, archive the run before stopping
Log a clear warning to the user that the branch no longer exists
Build passes with npm run build
userStories.notes: This prevents the agent from running on a ghost branch. The branch check should happen before any iteration starts.

userStories.title: Update agent-cli.md with new features documentation
userStories.description: Update agent-cli.md (the prompt file sent to the AI tool) to document the new features added by US-008 through US-010: the status command, prd.json schema validation, and stale branch detection/cleanup. The agent needs to know about these capabilities so it can use them correctly when working on stories. This task must be executed last, after all other new stories are complete, so the documentation reflects the final state of the codebase.
acceptanceCriteria: Update agent-cli.md to document the `status` subcommand (US-008)
Document prd.json schema validation and how it works (US-009)
Document stale branch detection behavior and what happens to prd.json/progress.txt (US-010)
Update the Architecture section if any new modules were added
Ensure the prompt file accurately reflects the current codebase capabilities
Build passes with npm run build
userStories.notes: All features documented: status command, schema validation, stale branch detection, file change tracking, progress logging, --init, --dry-run, --stories flags.

userStories.title: Add --stories flag to control how many stories the agent executes per run
userStories.description: Add a --stories <number> CLI flag that lets the user choose how many stories to execute in a single run. If the flag is not provided or the value doesn't exist, the agent behaves as it does now — executes one story per iteration, iterating until max_iterations is reached or all stories are complete. If --stories 3 is passed, the agent should stop after completing 3 stories (or earlier if max_iterations is hit or all stories are done). This gives users control over how much work the agent does per run.
acceptanceCriteria: Add --stories <number> flag to the CLI in src/index.ts via commander
If --stories is not provided, behavior is unchanged — iterate normally until max_iterations or all complete
If --stories N is provided, stop the loop after N stories have been completed in this run
The count is per-run, not total — if 2 stories were already complete and --stories 3 is passed, complete up to 3 more
Log how many stories were completed vs requested when the loop stops
Respect max_iterations as the hard upper bound — even if --stories 10 is passed, stop at max_iterations
Build passes with npm run build
userStories.notes: Default behavior (no flag) must remain exactly as it is today — one story per iteration, looping until done or max_iterations.

userStories.title: Stream real-time claude output during iterations instead of silent waiting
userStories.description: When running claude as the tool, the user currently sees only the iteration header and then nothing until the iteration finishes. Switch claude to use --output-format stream-json --include-partial-messages --verbose flags so that each line of stdout is a JSON event. Parse these events in real-time and display: text deltas (assistant response), tool use names and inputs, and completion status. This replaces the current silent wait with live feedback showing what the agent is doing. The completion signal (<promise>COMPLETE</promise>) should be detected in the final 'result' event's result field. Also extract and log cost/duration from the result event. For amp or other tools, keep current behavior unchanged.
acceptanceCriteria: When tool is 'claude', add --output-format stream-json --include-partial-messages --verbose to the command args
Parse stdout line-by-line as JSON — each line is a separate event object
Display text_delta events in real-time (print the delta text directly to stdout)
When content_block_start has type 'tool_use', show the tool name being called (e.g. 'Using: Read package.json')
When input_json_delta arrives, show the tool input being constructed
Detect completion by checking the 'result' event (type='result') for <promise>COMPLETE</promise> in its result field
Extract and log total_cost_usd and duration_ms from the result event
For amp tool, keep current behavior — no changes
Backward compatible: existing stdout capture for completion detection still works as fallback
Build passes with npm run build
userStories.notes: Investigation showed: stream-json requires --verbose flag. Events: system (init), stream_event (real-time deltas), assistant (complete message), user (tool results), result (final). Key stream_event.event types: content_block_start (tool_use detection), content_block_delta (text_delta for text, thinking_delta for thinking, input_json_delta for tool input).

userStories.title: Display status command output as a formatted table
userStories.description: Replace the current plain-text status output with a formatted table that shows all stories (completed and pending) in a single view. Use chalk for colors: green checkmark for completed stories, yellow circle for pending. Columns: Status, ID, Priority, Title, Acceptance Criteria count. Add a summary row at the bottom with totals. Sort by priority within each group (completed first, then pending). Keep the --dir flag working as-is. Use only chalk and Node built-ins — no external table library.
acceptanceCriteria: Replace current plain-text status output with a table showing all stories
Table columns: Status (icon), ID, Priority, Title, Acceptance Criteria (count)
Completed stories show green checkmark, pending stories show yellow circle
Sort completed stories first (by priority), then pending stories (by priority)
Summary row at bottom shows total completed vs pending counts
Keep --dir flag working unchanged
No external table library — use chalk and manual formatting only
Build passes with npm run build
userStories.notes: Replaced plain-text output with formatted table using chalk. Shows all stories sorted by completion status then priority, with summary row.
